headline: Europe writes down a peace of nations while China writes down a change of dynasty
summary: In October 1648 the Peace of Westphalia signed in the Rhineland cities of Osnabrück and Münster ended the Thirty Years' War, recognised the sovereignty of the Dutch Republic and the Swiss Confederacy, admitted Calvinism as a lawful confession, and set European statecraft on its long path toward a system of confessional-neutral sovereign states. The same year saw the Fronde erupt in Paris against the child-king Louis XIV; Charles I of England cornered in the Second Civil War; Cossacks under Bohdan Khmelnytsky in bloody revolt across Ukraine; a boy-sultan Mehmed IV enthroned in Istanbul after the strangling of his father Ibrahim; the Manchu Qing under regent Dorgon consolidating northern China as Ming loyalist courts fled south; and in Shahjahanabad the completion of Shah Jahan's new imperial capital.

snapshots.text: On 24 October 1648 the Treaties of Osnabrück (between the Holy Roman Empire and Sweden with their Protestant allies) and Münster (between the Empire and France with their Catholic allies) were signed, ending the Thirty Years' War that had killed perhaps four and a half to eight million people across Central Europe. Emperor Ferdinand III, Queen Christina of Sweden's plenipotentiary Johan Oxenstierna, and diplomats of Louis XIV's regent-minister Cardinal Mazarin agreed the settlement in parallel. The peace recognised Calvinism alongside Lutheranism and Catholicism, extended the 1555 principle of cuius regio, eius religio while adding subject protections, confirmed the sovereignty of the Dutch Republic (whose independence Spain had already accepted at Münster on 30 January) and of the Swiss Confederacy, redrew borders in favour of Sweden (Western Pomerania, Bremen-Verden) and France (Alsatian territories, Metz-Toul-Verdun), and formally recognised the three-hundred German states as sovereign polities. In Paris the arrest of the parlementaire Pierre Broussel on 26 August triggered the first day of barricades and the beginning of the Fronde, a five-year revolt of parlement and princes against Mazarin and the young Louis XIV. In England Parliament and Charles I fought a short Second Civil War, culminating in Cromwell's victory at Preston (17-19 August) and Colonel Pride's Purge of Parliament on 6 December, prelude to the king's execution the following January. In Ukraine Bohdan Khmelnytsky's Cossack uprising, ignited in January, defeated Polish armies at Zhovti Vody (May), Korsun (May), and Pyliavtsi (September); King Władysław IV of Poland died on 20 May and John II Casimir was elected in November. In Istanbul Sultan Ibrahim was deposed and strangled on 8 August; his seven-year-old son Mehmed IV succeeded.

In China the Ming-Qing transition was in its fifth year and still deeply contested. The Manchu Qing regime, ruling from Beijing since 1644 under the regency of Prince Dorgon on behalf of the child Shunzhi Emperor (r. 1643/44-1661), controlled all north China; the peasant rebel Li Zicheng, whose brief Shun dynasty had overthrown the last Ming emperor Chongzhen, had been dead since 1645, and his fellow rebel Zhang Xianzhong of the Xi dynasty in Sichuan had been killed by Qing troops in early 1647. The Southern Ming was fragmented into successive pretender courts: the Longwu emperor had been captured in 1646, and the Yongli emperor (Prince of Gui, Zhu Youlang, r. 1646-1662) was ruling the fugitive court from Guangdong and Guangxi, fleeing westward as Qing armies advanced. The Jiangnan region had been forcibly subjected to the Manchu queue-and-shave order in 1645, producing brutal massacres at Yangzhou and Jiading; by 1648 Han bannermen made up perhaps three-quarters of Qing forces. Along the southeast coast the Ming loyalist commander Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga) was building the naval base at Xiamen that would eventually take Taiwan from the Dutch in 1662. In Japan the Tokugawa shogun Iemitsu (r. 1623-1651) presided from Edo over a state locked down under the sakoku 'closed country' edicts of 1633-1639 that expelled Portuguese Catholics, restricted Dutch trade to Dejima islet in Nagasaki, and forbade Japanese from leaving; Emperor Go-Kōmyō reigned in Kyoto. In Korea King Injo, weakened by the humiliating Qing invasion of 1636-1637, was in the last year of his reign.

The Ottoman Empire in 1648 was in the deep dysfunction of what later historians would call the Sultanate of Women and the Köprülü era yet to come. On 8 August Sultan Ibrahim I ('the Mad'), whose neurotic and profligate reign of eight years had drained the treasury and alienated the janissaries and ulema, was deposed by a coalition of viziers, jurists, and army officers; he was strangled ten days later. His seven-year-old son Mehmed IV was enthroned on the same day and would reign until 1687 under a succession of powerful regents including his grandmother Kösem Sultan (murdered 1651) and his mother Turhan Hatice. The war for Crete against Venice, begun in 1645, ground on inconclusively; the Ottoman fleet suffered defeats in the Aegean. In Baghdad, retaken from the Safavids by Murad IV in 1638, Ottoman rule was stable. In Safavid Persia Shah Abbas II (r. 1642-1666), succeeded 1642 as a boy, was in his teens and beginning a personal reign of relative stability from the imperial capital of Isfahan; the Uzbeks pressed the north-east frontier, and the Mughal Shah Jahan retook Kandahar from the Safavids in a costly campaign in 1648-1649. In Cairo the local Mamluk beys continued to rule under nominal Ottoman governors; the port of Alexandria and the Red Sea coffee trade from Mocha flourished. In Arabia the Zaydi imamate of Yemen under al-Mu'ayyad Muhammad had expelled the Ottomans in 1636 and controlled the coffee ports and Sanaa.

Mughal India in 1648 was at the apex of Shah Jahan's reign. On 19 April 1648, after ten years of construction, Shah Jahan formally inaugurated Shahjahanabad, the new imperial capital in Delhi built on the west bank of the Yamuna beside the old Sultanate cities; the Red Fort (Lal Qila) with its Diwan-i-Am and Diwan-i-Khas, the great Jama Masjid (completed 1656), and a walled city of merchant houses and mosques replaced Agra as the seat of empire. The Taj Mahal at Agra, begun in 1632 as the tomb of the empress Mumtaz Mahal, was essentially complete. The Mughal army in this year, contemporary estimates suggest, comprised nearly a million infantry and cavalry; the empire's share of world GDP was climbing past twenty percent. Shah Jahan's four sons — Dara Shikoh (the eclectic Sufi and translator of the Upanishads to Persian, favoured heir at court), Shah Shuja (governor of Bengal), Aurangzeb (viceroy of the Deccan), and Murad Baksh (Malwa) — had begun the manoeuvres that would explode into the war of succession a decade later. In 1648-1649 Aurangzeb led a costly Mughal army into Central Asia, retaking Kandahar temporarily from the Safavids. In peninsular India the Deccan sultanates of Bijapur and Golconda paid Mughal tribute, and the young Maratha chief Shivaji (b. 1630) was capturing his first hill forts. Portuguese and Dutch factories, and the recently arrived English East India Company, competed for the textile, saltpetre, and indigo trades from the coasts. Ceylon (Sri Lanka) was in the middle of the Dutch-Portuguese war; the VOC would take Colombo in 1656.

In the western Sudan the Songhai empire had been broken since the Moroccan invasion of 1591; a fragmented Sa'dian Moroccan pashalik still nominally ruled Timbuktu and Gao, though real power was passing to Tuareg and Fulani lineages. The successor Songhai state (the Dendi kingdom) held out in the south. The Bambara kingdoms of Segou and Kaarta were forming on the middle Niger. In the central Sudan Kanem-Bornu under Mai Ali ibn al-Hajj Umar (r. 1645-1685) was in a period of prosperity from its capital at Ngazargamu, integrating scholarship at Katsina and Kano into the great trans-Saharan intellectual network. The Hausa city-states — Kano, Katsina, Zaria — continued their commerce in cotton textiles and leather. In the Ethiopian highlands Emperor Fasilides (r. 1632-1667), founder of the Gondar dynasty, had built the great castles of the new capital Gondar and expelled the Portuguese Jesuits in 1633, returning the empire to its Miaphysite Orthodox tradition. In the Kongo kingdom under King Garcia II Nkanga a Lukeni, the confrontation with the Portuguese slaving state of Angola (Luanda, founded 1575) was mounting toward the battle of Mbwila in 1665. Along the East African coast the Sultanate of Oman was preparing to expel the Portuguese from Mombasa (accomplished 1698). At the southern tip of Africa, the Dutch VOC had not yet established Cape Town — that would come in 1652 under Jan van Riebeeck — though Dutch, English, and Portuguese ships regularly watered at Table Bay. Trans-Atlantic slaving from Angola, Kongo, and the Bight of Benin to Brazil and the Caribbean was expanding rapidly.

The Americas in 1648 were increasingly, if unevenly, a colonial world. In the Caribbean the sugar revolution was under way: on Barbados, colonised by the English in 1627, planters had by the 1640s converted the island into a slave-based sugar economy modelled on Dutch expertise from the recently lost Dutch Brazil (Recife had fallen back to the Portuguese in the mid-1640s and would be formally recovered in 1654). The Spanish viceroyalties of New Spain (Mexico City) and Peru (Lima) governed vast populations of Indigenous and mestizo subjects under the encomienda, mita, and repartimiento labour systems; silver from Potosí and Zacatecas flowed to Seville and thence to Ming China through the Manila galleon. Portuguese Brazil under Governor-General Antônio Teles de Meneses at Salvador da Bahia was still fighting the Dutch WIC in Pernambuco; the Palmares quilombo of runaway enslaved people in the Alagoas backlands, founded around 1605, would resist a series of assaults until 1694. In North America the English colonies were consolidating: Massachusetts Bay under John Winthrop's successors; Virginia; the newly founded Maryland (1634) and Rhode Island (1636); the Dutch New Netherland at New Amsterdam under Peter Stuyvesant (from 1647); New France along the St Lawrence under governors of the Compagnie des Cent-Associés, in the middle of the Beaver Wars in which the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois Confederacy) devastated the Huron-Wendat in 1648-1649. The Spanish missions of New Mexico under Franciscan orders governed uneasily among the Pueblo peoples. Indigenous depopulation from epidemic disease continued at appalling rates almost everywhere colonisers reached.

In the Pacific of 1648 European penetration was still shallow. Two years earlier, in 1642-1643, the Dutch VOC navigator Abel Tasman had sighted Van Diemen's Land (Tasmania), the west coast of the South Island of Aotearoa (New Zealand — his brief violent encounter at Golden Bay was Māori-European first contact), and Tonga and Fiji, without pursuing colonial follow-up. Spanish Manila-Acapulco galleons crossed the north Pacific once a year and had planted a tenuous colonial administration in Guam and the northern Marianas from 1668 (still ahead in 1648). The Māori iwi of Aotearoa continued their pā-and-hapū life; on Rapa Nui the great ahu-and-moai construction was near or past its peak and the island's ecological system was under strain from centuries of deforestation and pressure on seabird colonies. The Hawaiian chiefdoms consolidated on each of the main islands under lineages that would eventually be unified by Kamehameha at the close of the eighteenth century. Across western Polynesia the Tu'i Tonga confederation continued long-standing maritime networks with Samoa and Fiji. In Australia hundreds of distinct Aboriginal nations maintained songline-based land management and ceremony as they had for tens of thousands of years; along the northern coast a well-established seasonal Makassan trepang-fishing contact from Sulawesi brought iron, tobacco, and cloth in exchange for sea cucumber. In the New Guinea highlands intensively drained horticultural systems supported dense populations, and on the coasts Austronesian-speaking peoples sustained the Kula-type exchange networks of the Massim.
